distraction.definition: something that takes your attention away from what you are doing or feeling
distraction.example: The toy was a distraction from the child's real feelings.

tactic.definition: a planned way of doing something to get a result
tactic.example: Distraction is a tactic many parents use with children.

favoured.definition: liked or preferred more than others
favoured.example: Distraction is a tactic favoured by many parents.

divert.definition: to turn someone's attention away to something else
divert.example: She tried to divert the child from crying by showing him a toy.

commonly.definition: done by many people, happening often
commonly.example: This method is commonly used but rarely helpful.

rarely.example: Distraction is commonly used but rarely appropriate.

appropriate.definition: right or suitable for a particular situation
appropriate.example: Distraction is rarely the appropriate response to a child's feelings.

manipulated.definition: controlled or influenced in a clever but unfair way
manipulated.example: Being manipulated will not help your child grow.

sincerity.definition: being honest and truly meaning what you say
sincerity.example: Look into a baby's eyes and you will see nothing but sincerity.

deserve.definition: to have earned the right to receive something
deserve.example: Our children deserve sincerity from us.

sincere.definition: honest and really meaning what you say or do
sincere.example: Distraction is not sincere on the part of the parent.

manipulative.definition: using clever tricks to control people in an unfair way
manipulative.example: Using distraction as a manipulative tactic is not honest.

insult.definition: something that shows no respect, that is rude
insult.example: Distraction can be an insult to a child's intelligence.

intelligence.definition: the ability to learn, understand, and think clearly
intelligence.example: Do not insult your child's intelligence by trying to trick them.

convey.definition: to communicate or express a message or feeling
convey.example: What message does distraction convey to the child?

graze.definition: to scrape the skin so it becomes raw and sore
graze.example: She fell and badly grazed her knee on the ground.

embarrassment.definition: a feeling of being ashamed or uncomfortable in front of others
embarrassment.example: She felt embarrassment after falling in public.

injection.definition: when medicine is put into your body through a needle
injection.example: The child was scared of getting an injection.

sensation.definition: a physical feeling you get through your body
sensation.example: Focus on the sensation of my fingers on your forehead.

stroking.definition: moving your hand gently over something
stroking.example: The gentle stroking of her forehead helped calm her.

forehead.definition: the flat area of your face above your eyes
forehead.example: She stroked the child's forehead to comfort him.

comfort.definition: making someone feel better when they are sad or in pain
comfort.example: She offered distraction as a comfort during the medical procedure.

liable.example: Children are liable to treat you the way you treat them.

manner.definition: the way you do something or behave
manner.example: Your children will treat you in whatever manner you treat them.

nursery.definition: a place where young children are cared for while parents work
nursery.example: Tell your child's nursery teachers about your preferences.

childminders.definition: people who are paid to look after children in their own home
childminders.example: Let your childminders know how you want feelings handled.

empathized.definition: understood and shared another person's feelings
empathized.example: The child's feelings should be empathized with, not ignored.

ward.definition: to keep something away, to prevent it
ward.example: Distracting a child to ward off a conflict does not teach them.

conflict.definition: a fight or disagreement between people
conflict.example: Avoiding conflict does not help children learn to deal with it.

negotiate.definition: to talk and try to reach an agreement
negotiate.example: Children need to learn how to negotiate a struggle.

struggle.definition: a difficult situation that takes effort to deal with
struggle.example: Learning to negotiate a struggle is an important skill.

temporarily.definition: for a short time only, not forever
temporarily.example: The child was only temporarily distracted from wanting the key.

frustration.definition: the feeling of being upset because you cannot do or have what you want
frustration.example: You can help them with their frustration instead of hiding it.

furious.example: I can hear you are furious about not getting the key.

contain.definition: to hold something inside, to keep it under control
contain.example: If you stay calm and contain your child's feelings, they learn too.

internalize.definition: to learn something so deeply it becomes part of who you are
internalize.example: The time invested will help them internalize these skills.

repeatedly.definition: done many times, again and again
repeatedly.example: If you repeatedly distract your child, they lose focus ability.

unwittingly.example: You are unwittingly teaching them not to concentrate.

discouraging.definition: making someone less willing or confident to do something
discouraging.example: Distraction is discouraging them from paying attention to hard things.

concentrate.definition: to focus your mind on one thing
concentrate.example: Children need to learn to concentrate on difficult tasks.

bar.definition: something that prevents or blocks something else
bar.example: Unwanted distraction is a bar to a close relationship.

tempted.definition: feeling like you want to do something, especially something you should not
tempted.example: You might be tempted to distract them instead of listening.

toddler.definition: a very young child who has just learned to walk
toddler.example: For a toddler, a parent leaving can feel like the end of the world.

inconsolable.definition: so sad that nothing can make you feel better
inconsolable.example: The toddler seemed inconsolable when his mother left.

sneak.definition: to leave quietly so no one notices
sneak.example: It is important not to sneak out when leaving your child.

overdramatic.definition: showing too much emotion, more than the situation needs
overdramatic.example: Being overdramatic about leaving will not help the child.

mirror.definition: here it means reflecting back what someone feels so they can see it
mirror.example: Parents need to be the mirror for their child's feelings.

attentive.definition: paying close attention, watching and listening carefully
attentive.example: Be attentive and mindful about your own discomfort.

mindful.definition: being aware of and paying attention to what is happening now
mindful.example: Be mindful about your own feelings while caring for the child.

overreact.definition: to show too strong a reaction to something
overreact.example: Be concerned but do not overreact to the child's crying.

resigned.definition: accepting something you cannot change, even if you do not like it
resigned.example: Once you become resigned to the situation, you feel more open.

distress.definition: a strong feeling of sadness, pain, or worry
distress.example: Do not distract someone while they are deep in their distress.

heartfelt.definition: deeply and truly felt, coming from the heart
heartfelt.example: Do not push someone's heartfelt feelings aside.

intense.definition: very strong in feeling or force
intense.example: Children's feelings are just as intense as adult feelings.

observe.definition: to watch carefully and notice things
observe.example: A child can learn to observe their feelings over time.

containing.definition: keeping something under control inside yourself
containing.example: Containing feelings is a skill children must learn.

mental.definition: related to the mind and how you think and feel
mental.example: Good mental health means accepting all your feelings.

acceptable.definition: allowed or considered okay by others
acceptable.example: Children need to learn acceptable ways of showing their feelings.

essential.example: It is essential to accept our children's feelings.

process.definition: to think about and deal with something step by step
process.example: Putting feelings into words helps children process them.

procedure.definition: a medical treatment or operation done by a doctor
procedure.example: If your child needs a medical procedure, explain what will happen.

optimistic.example: Leave your child with a calm and optimistic attitude.

panic.definition: a sudden strong feeling of fear that makes you unable to think clearly
panic.example: If you panic about going, your child will feel more afraid.

distorted.definition: changed from its true shape or meaning, twisted
distorted.example: Lying about time will give the child a distorted idea.

occasion.definition: a particular time when something happens
occasion.example: The child will not believe you on the next occasion.

shush.definition: to tell someone to be quiet by saying 'shh'
shush.example: Do not shush the child or tell them to stop crying.

firm.definition: strong and sure in what you say or do, not easily changed
firm.example: Be calm and firm when leaving your child.
